slides.title: Python Asks Yes-or-No Questions
slides.body: A **comparison operator** puts two values side by side and asks a question. The answer is always **`True`** or **`False`** тАФ no maybes.
slides.tip: True and False are called booleans тАФ they're the foundation of every decision your program makes.

slides.title: Six Operators, One Job
slides.body: Python has six comparison operators: `==` `!=` `<` `>` `<=` `>=`. Each one sits between two values and returns a boolean.
slides.code:
print(5 == 5)   # equal to?
print(5 != 3)   # not equal?
print(4 < 10)   # less than?
print(7 > 9)    # greater than?
slides.tip: Don't confuse = (assignment) with == (comparison) тАФ one stores, the other asks.

slides.title: Numbers Compare Like Math Class
slides.body: Integers, floats, or a mix тАФ Python handles them all. Store a comparison result in a variable just like any value.
slides.tip: 5 == 5.0 is True тАФ Python compares values, not types.

slides.title: Strings Compare Like a Dictionary
slides.body: String equality is **case-sensitive**. You can also use `<` and `>` тАФ Python compares strings letter by letter, like alphabetical order.
slides.code:
print("Apple" == "apple")   # capital A matters!
print("apple" < "banana")   # 'a' before 'b'
slides.tip: "cat" < "cats" is True тАФ the shorter word comes first when everything else matches.

slides.title: Chain Comparisons Like Math
slides.body: Python lets you chain comparisons in one line тАФ perfect for checking if a value falls inside a range.
slides.code:
x = 7
print(1 < x < 10)    # between 1 and 10?
print(0 <= x <= 5)   # between 0 and 5?
slides.tip: 1 < x < 10 is cleaner than writing x > 1 and x < 10 separately.

slides.title: You've Got Comparisons!
slides.body: Six operators, always `True` or `False`. You can compare numbers, strings, and even chain them тАФ and you know not to mix up `=` with `==`.
slides.tip: Use == to compare values. Use is only with None. You're ready to make decisions in code!
